cards.front: Smallest stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time
Minimum difference between two stimuli detectable 50% of the time
Law stating the JND is a constant proportion of the original stimulus
Stimulus intensity below the absolute threshold of conscious awareness
Theory that detecting a stimulus depends on both intensity and the observer's decision criteria
In signal detection, correctly detecting a stimulus that is present
In signal detection, reporting a stimulus when none is present
In signal detection, failing to detect a stimulus that is present
Signal detection term for an observer's tendency to say 'yes' regardless of the stimulus
Decline in sensory response to a constant, unchanging stimulus
Conversion of physical stimulus energy into neural signals
Photoreceptors responsible for color vision and fine detail in bright light
Photoreceptors responsible for vision in dim light and peripheral vision
Theory that color vision arises from three cone types (red, green, blue)
Theory explaining color afterimages via red-green, blue-yellow, black-white pairs
Inner-ear structure containing hair cells that transduce sound
Cells in the cochlea that convert sound vibrations into neural signals
Likely deficit if hair cells are missing in only part of the cochlea
Theory that pitch is coded by the location of hair cell stimulation on the basilar membrane
Theory that pitch is coded by the firing rate matching the sound's frequency
Inner-ear structures that sense rotational movement of the head
Sense of body position and movement of limbs
The five basic taste qualities
Processing that starts with raw sensory data and builds up to perception
Processing driven by prior knowledge, expectations, and context
School of psychology emphasizing that the whole perception exceeds the sum of parts
Gestalt principle of grouping nearby elements together
Gestalt principle of grouping elements that look alike
Gestalt principle of filling gaps to perceive a complete figure
Perceiving objects as stable in size, shape, and color despite changing sensory input
Depth cue requiring both eyes, based on retinal image differences
Monocular depth cue in which nearer objects block farther ones
Focusing attention on one stimulus while filtering others, as at a noisy party
Learning in which a neutral stimulus comes to elicit a reflexive response
In classical conditioning, the stimulus that naturally elicits a response
In classical conditioning, the reflexive, unlearned reaction to the US
In classical conditioning, a previously neutral stimulus that now triggers a learned response
In classical conditioning, the learned response to the conditioned stimulus
Pairing the neutral stimulus with the US to establish a conditioned response
Weakening of a conditioned response when the CS is presented without the US
Reappearance of an extinguished response after a rest period
Responding to stimuli similar to the original conditioned stimulus
Researcher famous for classical conditioning experiments with dogs
Learning in which behavior is shaped by its consequences
Psychologist who formulated the law of effect and operant conditioning
cards.back: B. F. Skinner (law of effect: Edward Thorndike)

cards.front: Consequence that increases the likelihood of a behavior
Consequence that decreases the likelihood of a behavior
Adding a pleasant stimulus to increase a behavior
Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase a behavior
Adding an aversive stimulus to decrease a behavior
Removing a desirable stimulus to decrease a behavior
Reinforcer that gains value through association, such as money or tokens
Schedule reinforcing after a set number of responses
Schedule reinforcing after an unpredictable number of responses
Schedule reinforcing the first response after a fixed time interval
Reinforcement schedule producing the highest, most resistant response rate
Learning by watching and imitating others
Psychologist known for the Bobo doll observational-learning study
Behavior therapy using tokens as secondary reinforcers to shape behavior
Memory store holding sensory input for a fraction of a second
Memory store holding information about 15-30 seconds without rehearsal
Approximate capacity of short-term memory in items
Grouping items into meaningful units to expand short-term capacity
Memory system that both stores and manipulates information for current tasks
Memory store with essentially unlimited capacity and duration
Linking new information to existing knowledge for durable storage
Memory type for facts and events that can be consciously recalled
Explicit memory for general facts and knowledge
Explicit memory for personally experienced events
Memory type for skills and conditioned responses, recalled unconsciously
Implicit memory for motor skills such as riding a bicycle
Retrieving information without external cues
Identifying previously learned information among options
Tendency to remember items at the start and end of a list best
Enhanced memory for items at the beginning of a list
Enhanced memory for items at the end of a list
Better recall for information relevant to oneself
Curve describing decline of memory retention over time
Old information disrupting the recall of newly learned information
New information disrupting the recall of previously learned information
Lasting strengthening of synaptic connections thought to underlie memory
Inability to form new memories after brain injury
Inability to recall memories formed before a brain injury
Progressive neurodegenerative disease causing severe memory loss and cortical atrophy
Mental shortcut or rule of thumb for solving problems quickly
Step-by-step procedure guaranteeing a correct solution
Judging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind
Judging probability by resemblance to a prototype or stereotype
Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered
Seeking information that supports existing beliefs
Overestimating one's ability to have predicted an outcome after it occurs
Inability to see a novel use for an object beyond its typical function
Tendency to solve problems using previously successful methods
Intelligence based on accumulated knowledge and facts
Intelligence based on reasoning and solving novel problems
Psychologist who proposed a theory of multiple intelligences
Smallest unit of sound in a language
Smallest unit of meaning in a language
Hypothesis that language shapes thought and perception
Theorist who argued language is learned through operant conditioning
Theorist who proposed an innate language acquisition device
Optimal window in development for acquiring language
General state of awareness of oneself and the environment
Brain waves characteristic of an alert, awake state
Low-frequency, high-amplitude brain waves of deep (slow-wave) sleep
Sleep stage characterized by sleep spindles and K-complexes
Sleep stage dominated by delta waves, also called slow-wave sleep
Sleep stage of vivid dreaming, rapid eye movement, and muscle paralysis
Brain-wave feature that makes REM sleep resemble wakefulness
Sleep disorder of walking or activity during slow-wave sleep
Theory that dreams are the cortex interpreting random brainstem activity

Drugs that distort perception and cause hallucinations, such as LSD
Dopaminergic pathway most associated with drug reward and addiction

Rogers' concept of accepting a person without conditions

Kohlberg's level based on abstract ethical principles
